description:
You are a network engineer who just collected a mixed batch of raw ping outputs  
    into the file  
        /home/user/raw_logs/ping_results.log  

    Your goal is to quickly transform that unstructured text into two concise, machine-readable
    artifacts that can be shared with the NOC and fed to automated dashboards.

    1) Create the directory  
           /home/user/output  
       if it does not yet exist.

    2) Parse /home/user/raw_logs/ping_results.log and build a CSV file named  
           /home/user/output/summary.csv  

       Exact CSV format that the automated grader will check:

       • The first line **must** be the header below, written exactly as shown  
         (case-sensitive, no extra spaces):  

             host,ip,tx,rx,loss_pct,avg_rtt_ms  

       • One data row per host must follow, sorted **alphabetically by the host
         column** (ascending).  
       • Each column has the following meaning and formatting rules:  
           host        – hostname as it appears in the first “PING …” line.  
           ip          – the IP (or IPv6) address that appears in parentheses on that same line.  
           tx          – packets transmitted (integer).  
           rx          – packets received    (integer).  
           loss_pct    – packet-loss percentage (integer, no % sign, no decimals).  
           avg_rtt_ms  – the *average* RTT value taken from the “rtt … avg …” line,  
                         printed with exactly three (3) digits after the decimal point  
                         (e.g. 12.345).  
       • Fields are delimited with a single comma; no spaces are allowed around commas.  
       • Example of a single, correctly-formatted data row (do **not** copy verbatim –  
         just for illustration):  

             myhost,192.0.2.10,4,4,0,12.345  

    3) Generate an alert list called  
           /home/user/output/alerts.log  

       Every host whose  
           loss_pct  > 20   OR  
           avg_rtt_ms > 100  
       must appear here, one per line, using **exactly** this template:  

           ALERT: <host> (<ip>) - loss=<LOSS>% , avg_rtt=<AVG>ms  

       where  
         • <LOSS> is the same integer you wrote in the CSV (no decimals),  
         • <AVG>  is the average RTT with exactly three decimal places **and** the trailing “ms”.  
       The file itself must be sorted alphabetically by host, just like the CSV.  
       Example alert line (again, for illustration only):  

           ALERT: core_router (198.51.100.1) - loss=75%, avg_rtt=123.456ms  

    4) Ensure that both /home/user/output/summary.csv and /home/user/output/alerts.log  
       are world-readable (chmod 644 or equivalent) so automated tests can open them.

    5) Accomplish all of the above using standard shell utilities that ship with a
       minimal Linux installation (e.g. grep, awk, sed, cut, sort, printf, cat).
       A concise pipeline of roughly 5-10 commands is expected.

    The grader will fail the task if:
       • Either output file is missing or in the wrong location.  
       • Header, column order, delimiter spacing, or number formatting deviate from spec.  
       • Rows are not sorted alphabetically by the host column.  
       • Alerts contain hosts that should not be there, omit hosts that should,  
         or do not follow the exact text template.  

    No external scripting languages (Python, Perl, etc.) are necessary—stick to the shell.
    When finished, you can cat both resulting files to confirm their contents.
truth:
Initial state (already present before the agent begins):

    1. Directory /home/user/raw_logs exists with 755 permissions.

    2. File /home/user/raw_logs/ping_results.log has the following **exact** content
       (blank lines between host blocks are intentional and must remain):

PING google.com (172.217.164.110) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 172.217.164.110: icmp_seq=1 ttl=115 time=14.3 ms
64 bytes from 172.217.164.110: icmp_seq=2 ttl=115 time=15.8 ms
64 bytes from 172.217.164.110: icmp_seq=3 ttl=115 time=16.1 ms
64 bytes from 172.217.164.110: icmp_seq=4 ttl=115 time=15.7 ms

--- 172.217.164.110 ping statistics ---
4 packets transmitted, 4 received, 0% packet loss, time 3005ms
rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 14.123/15.456/17.890/1.234 ms


PING cloudflare.com (104.16.123.96) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 104.16.123.96: icmp_seq=1 ttl=59 time=22.4 ms
64 bytes from 104.16.123.96: icmp_seq=2 ttl=59 time=23.1 ms
64 bytes from 104.16.123.96: icmp_seq=3 ttl=59 time=22.8 ms
64 bytes from 104.16.123.96: icmp_seq=4 ttl=59 time=22.5 ms

--- 104.16.123.96 ping statistics ---
4 packets transmitted, 4 received, 0% packet loss, time 4005ms
rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 21.978/22.678/23.145/0.421 ms


PING fileserver (10.0.0.5) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 10.0.0.5: icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=82.1 ms
64 bytes from 10.0.0.5: icmp_seq=2 ttl=64 time=85.4 ms
64 bytes from 10.0.0.5: icmp_seq=3 ttl=64 time=88.2 ms

--- 10.0.0.5 ping statistics ---
4 packets transmitted, 3 received, 25% packet loss, time 4007ms
rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 80.567/85.234/89.105/3.456 ms


PING remote_site (203.0.113.25) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 203.0.113.25: icmp_seq=2 ttl=46 time=109.9 ms
64 bytes from 203.0.113.25: icmp_seq=4 ttl=46 time=110.8 ms

--- 203.0.113.25 ping statistics ---
4 packets transmitted, 2 received, 50% packet loss, time 3998ms
rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 109.255/110.345/110.831/0.788 ms


PING ipv6_google (2001:4860:4860::8888) 56(84) bytes of data.
64 bytes from 2001:4860:4860::8888: icmp_seq=1 ttl=115 time=27.1 ms
64 bytes from 2001:4860:4860::8888: icmp_seq=2 ttl=115 time=28.4 ms
64 bytes from 2001:4860:4860::8888: icmp_seq=3 ttl=115 time=27.9 ms
64 bytes from 2001:4860:4860::8888: icmp_seq=4 ttl=115 time=27.3 ms

--- 2001:4860:4860::8888 ping statistics ---
4 packets transmitted, 4 received, 0% packet loss, time 4006ms
rtt min/avg/max/mdev = 26.987/27.890/28.431/0.517 ms


    3. There is no /home/user/output directory when the scenario starts.

    Verification expectations after the agent finishes:

    A) /home/user/output/summary.csv must contain exactly:

host,ip,tx,rx,loss_pct,avg_rtt_ms
cloudflare.com,104.16.123.96,4,4,0,22.678
fileserver,10.0.0.5,4,3,25,85.234
google.com,172.217.164.110,4,4,0,15.456
ipv6_google,2001:4860:4860::8888,4,4,0,27.890
remote_site,203.0.113.25,4,2,50,110.345

    (Every newline is a Unix LF, no trailing blank line.)

    B) /home/user/output/alerts.log must contain exactly:

ALERT: fileserver (10.0.0.5) - loss=25%, avg_rtt=85.234ms
ALERT: remote_site (203.0.113.25) - loss=50%, avg_rtt=110.345ms

    (Alphabetical order, Unix LF line endings, no trailing blank line.)

    C) Both files must be present with mode 0644 or more permissive (e.g. 0664).

    The autograder will diff the two output files byte-for-byte against the
    expectations above and will also stat them for correct permissions.
